titleStmt.title: Letter from Sister Albeus Fogarty to Royal College of Physicians of Ireland, 2 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Sister M. Albeus Fogarty

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sister M. Albeus Fogarty to the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland (RCPI) informing the college that the hospital's treasurer will be unable to attend the conference regarding the establishment of an Irish college of nursing. Sister Fogarty adds that herself and her fellow nursing staff are in complete support of state registration for fully trained nurses and are now, more than ever, convinced of its necessity.Sister Albeus Fogarty (1868-1952) was a member of the Matrons' Council of Great Britain and Ireland and of the Executive Committee of the Irish Nurses' Association. She encouraged participation across all religious and lay sectors of nurses in order to create a truly national league of Irish nurses.

msIdentifier.repository: Royal College of Physicians of Ireland

correspAction.placeName: South Charitable Infirmary and County Hospital, Cork, Ireland
Royal College of Physicians, Kildare Street, Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
South Charitable Infirmary and County Hospital CORK, April 2 nd 1916 Dear Sir, Our Honorary
Treasurer is reluctantly
compelled to forego
pleasure of being
present at the Conference
on the 4 th inst. Permit me to add
the Nursing Staff & I
have always been
in favor of State
Registration & are
now, more than ever,
convinced of its
necessity. The time
has certainly come
when fully trained
Nurses should be
protected by the
State. I am, Dear Sir,
yours respectfully, Rs. M. A. Fogarty
Matron.
